# Kubernetes API Server Library: Subsystem Architecture and Extension Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

The `k8s.io/apiserver` library (`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`) is the reusable foundation upon which every Kubernetes API server is built. It is not the full `kube-apiserver` binary—it is the generic scaffolding that provides the HTTP serving infrastructure, request filtering pipeline, type-system integration, storage abstraction, and security plumbing. A consumer of this library instantiates a `GenericAPIServer`, attaches REST storage implementations behind `APIGroupInfo` descriptors, and gets a standards-compliant Kubernetes API surface for free.

### Upstream Dependencies

The library sits on top of:

- **`k8s.io/apimachinery`** — type system primitives (`runtime.Scheme`, `runtime.Object`, `GroupVersionKind`, codec infrastructure, field/label selectors)
- **`k8s.io/client-go`** — informers and the REST client used by the loopback client and optional webhook callers
- **etcd (via `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3`)** — the sole officially-supported storage backend, accessed through `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/etcd3/`
- **`k8s.io/kube-openapi`** — OpenAPI v2/v3 spec generation and serving

### Downstream Consumers

- **`kube-apiserver` core** (`pkg/controlplane/`) — registers built-in API groups by calling `GenericAPIServer.InstallLegacyAPIGroup` / `InstallAPIGroups`
- **`kube-aggregator`** — builds an aggregation-layer server on top of the library
- **`apiextensions-apiserver`** — uses the library to serve CRDs
- **Extension API servers** built with `apiserver-builder` or manually using `RecommendedOptions`

### Boundaries

The library handles everything between network bytes and your storage call: TLS termination, authentication, authorization, flow control, serialisation, admission, versioning, and watch fan-out. It does **not** own cluster-level policy (that is RBAC controller code in `pkg/`), controller logic, or the etcd cluster itself.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

The directory structure maps almost one-to-one to functional responsibility:

| Directory | Primary Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `pkg/server/` | Server bootstrap: `Config`, `GenericAPIServer`, handler chain wiring, lifecycle signals, hooks |
| `pkg/server/options/` | CLI flag binding; `RecommendedOptions` composes all option groups for extension API servers |
| `pkg/endpoints/` | HTTP-level dispatch: `APIInstaller`, verb handlers (`create.go`, `get.go`, `patch.go`, `watch.go`), filters |
| `pkg/registry/rest/` | REST storage interfaces (`rest.Storage`, `Getter`, `Creater`, `Updater`, etc.) |
| `pkg/registry/generic/registry/` | Generic `Store` struct implementing CRUD+Watch on top of `storage.Interface` |
| `pkg/storage/` | Storage interface definition and `Versioner` abstraction |
| `pkg/storage/cacher/` | In-memory watch cache (`Cacher`, `watchCache`, `cacheWatcher`) |
| `pkg/storage/etcd3/` | etcd3 implementation of `storage.Interface` |
| `pkg/admission/` | Admission plugin interfaces, chain, plugin registry, initializers |
| `pkg/authentication/` | Pluggable authenticator chain |
| `pkg/authorization/` | Pluggable authorizer chain |
| `pkg/audit/` | Policy-driven audit event pipeline |
| `pkg/util/flowcontrol/` | API Priority and Fairness (APF): `FlowSchema`, `PriorityLevel`, work estimator |

### The `GenericAPIServer` and `Config`

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` contains the `GenericAPIServer` struct: the central object at runtime. It owns the `APIServerHandler` (the full HTTP handler chain), the discovery endpoint managers, health check registries, the audit backend, and the `delegationTarget` pointer for the server chain.

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/config.go` holds the `Config` struct, which is populated from option structs and then "completed" (via `Config.Complete()`) before being used to construct the server. Default values are set in `NewConfig`:

```go
// config.go:427-478
return &Config{
    BuildHandlerChainFunc:          DefaultBuildHandlerChain,
    MaxRequestsInFlight:            400,
    MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight:    200,
    RequestTimeout:                 time.Duration(60) * time.Second,
    JSONPatchMaxCopyBytes:          int64(3 * 1024 * 1024),
    MaxRequestBodyBytes:            int64(3 * 1024 * 1024),
    ...
}
```

The field `BuildHandlerChainFunc` is the primary extension point for the filter pipeline—it defaults to `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` but callers may replace it entirely.

### `APIGroupInfo` and REST Storage Registration

An API group is attached to the server by constructing an `APIGroupInfo` and calling `InstallAPIGroups`:

```go
// genericapiserver.go:71-98
type APIGroupInfo struct {
    PrioritizedVersions          []schema.GroupVersion
    VersionedResourcesStorageMap map[string]map[string]rest.Storage
    Scheme                       *runtime.Scheme
    NegotiatedSerializer         runtime.NegotiatedSerializer
    ParameterCodec               runtime.ParameterCodec
    ...
}
```

The `VersionedResourcesStorageMap` maps `version → resource-name → rest.Storage`. `InstallAPIGroups` (`genericapiserver.go:876`) iterates the versions, calls `installAPIResources`, and registers discovery handlers. `InstallLegacyAPIGroup` does the same for the `/api` (core) prefix.

### The `Store` — Generic CRUD Engine

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go` contains the `Store` struct, which is the standard CRUD/Watch implementation. Every built-in resource embeds a `Store`. It delegates to a `DryRunnableStorage` (wrapping `storage.Interface`) and enforces business logic through strategy objects:

```go
// store.go:100-248 (selected fields)
type Store struct {
    CreateStrategy rest.RESTCreateStrategy
    UpdateStrategy rest.RESTUpdateStrategy
    DeleteStrategy rest.RESTDeleteStrategy
    BeginCreate    BeginCreateFunc   // optional "transaction" hook
    AfterCreate    AfterCreateFunc   // optional post-create hook
    BeginUpdate    BeginUpdateFunc
    AfterUpdate    AfterUpdateFunc
    AfterDelete    AfterDeleteFunc
    Decorator      func(runtime.Object) // exit hook after storage read
    Storage        DryRunnableStorage
    ...
}
```

Strategies carry the actual business logic (field immutability, name generation, namespace defaulting, validation). The `Store` itself is storage-agnostic; it calls the strategy before writing and after reading.

### The `APIServerHandler` — Routing Architecture

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/handler.go` describes the dual-routing system:

```
FullHandlerChain → Director → GoRestfulContainer (API paths)
                            → NonGoRestfulMux    (utility paths: /healthz, /metrics, /openapi)
```

`GoRestfulContainer` (from `github.com/emicklei/go-restful`) handles structured REST API routing with content negotiation. `NonGoRestfulMux` is a simple `PathRecorderMux` for everything else. The `Director` inspects the incoming path to decide which branch to use.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

This section traces a `kubectl create deployment` (a `POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments`) through the full stack.

### Phase 1: Outer Filter Chain (`DefaultBuildHandlerChain`)

The filter chain is assembled inside-out (wrappers applied in reverse order of the listed sequence). The code in `config.go:1015-1096` shows the exact wrap order. When a request arrives, it traverses these filters from outermost to innermost:

1. **`WithAuditInit`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit_init.go`) — allocates an audit event for the request.
2. **`WithPanicRecovery`** (`pkg/server/filters/wrap.go`) — catches panics, converts them to 500s.
3. **`WithMuxAndDiscoveryComplete`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/mux_discovery_complete.go`) — blocks requests until all API paths are installed (prevents spurious 404s at startup).
4. **`WithRequestReceivedTimestamp`** — stamps the request receipt time.
5. **`WithRequestInfo`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/requestinfo.go`) — parses the URL into a `RequestInfo` struct (verb, resource, namespace, name) and injects it into the context.
6. **`WithRoutine`** (optional, feature-gated) — executes downstream handlers in a new goroutine to minimize stack depth.
7. **`WithLatencyTrackers`** / **`WithHTTPLogging`** — metrics and structured logging.
8. **`WithRetryAfter`** (optional) — rejects new requests with HTTP 429 during graceful shutdown.
9. **`WithHSTS`** / **`WithCacheControl`** — response header policies.
10. **`WithProbabilisticGoaway`** (optional) — probabilistically sends `GOAWAY` on HTTP/2 connections to trigger client reconnection and load rebalancing.
11. **`WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown`** / **`WithWaitGroup`** — tracks in-flight requests for graceful drain; rate-limits watch draining during shutdown.
12. **`WithRequestDeadline`** — applies the request timeout from `Config.RequestTimeout` (default 60s) to the context.
13. **`WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests`** — enforces the deadline by running downstream in a goroutine and returning a timeout response if the deadline is exceeded.
14. **`WithWarningRecorder`** — thread-safe buffer for adding warning headers to the response.
15. **`WithAuthentication`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go`) — runs the authenticator chain; on failure, invokes the `failedHandler` which returns `401 Unauthorized`.
16. **`WithTracing`** (optional, feature-gated) — starts an OpenTelemetry span; placed after authn so clients can influence sampling.
17. **`WithAudit`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit.go`) — populates audit event with user identity and response status.
18. **`WithImpersonation`** / **`WithConstrainedImpersonation`** — checks and applies user impersonation (`Impersonate-*` headers).
19. **`WithPriorityAndFairness`** or **`WithMaxInFlightLimit`** — either APF-based flow control (classifies request into a `FlowSchema`/`PriorityLevel` queue) or the simpler fixed limit (`MaxRequestsInFlight=400`, `MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight=200`).
20. **`WithAuthorization`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go`) — invokes the authorizer chain; returns `403 Forbidden` on denial.

At this point the request is authenticated, authorized, and admitted through flow control. The body has still **not** been deserialized.

### Phase 2: REST Endpoint Handler (`pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go`)

The `APIInstaller` (`pkg/endpoints/installer.go`) registered an HTTP handler for `POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments` when `InstallAPIGroups` was called. That handler is `createHandler` in `pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go`.

Inside `createHandler`:

1. **Content negotiation** — selects the input and output serializer based on `Content-Type` / `Accept` headers (`negotiation.NegotiateInputSerializer`).
2. **Body read** — `limitedReadBodyWithRecordMetric` reads up to `MaxRequestBodyBytes` (3 MiB by default).
3. **Decode** — `scope.Serializer.DecoderToVersion(decodeSerializer, scope.HubGroupVersion)` converts the wire JSON/YAML/protobuf bytes into the internal version of the object via the `Scheme`'s hub-and-spoke conversion.
4. **Field validation** — if `FieldValidation=Warn` or `Strict`, a strict decoder is used and unknown fields produce warnings or errors.
5. **Admission (mutation phase)** — `admission.NewAttributesRecord(obj, ...)` creates the `Attributes` package; `requestFunc` wraps the final storage call. The field manager (`FieldManager.UpdateNoErrors`) sets `managedFields`.
6. **`finisher.FinishRequest`** — runs the actual storage call and admission in a dedicated goroutine with the request context deadline.
7. **Admission (validation phase)** — `ManagedFieldsValidatingAdmissionController` validates field manager annotations; then the generic admission chain's `Validate` pass runs.
8. **Storage write** — `r.Create(ctx, name, obj, validateFunc, options)` calls the `Store.Create` method, which invokes `CreateStrategy.PrepareForCreate`, `CreateStrategy.Validate`, then `Storage.Create` to write to etcd via `GuaranteedUpdate` (or the cacher).
9. **Response** — the stored object is serialized to the negotiated output format and written to `http.ResponseWriter`.

### Phase 3: Storage Layer

The storage call descends through:

```
Store.Storage (DryRunnableStorage)
  → cacher.Cacher (implements storage.Interface)
      → etcd3.store (implements storage.Interface)
          → etcd grpc client
```

For **writes**, the cacher delegates directly to the etcd3 store. For **reads and watches**, the cacher serves from its in-memory `watchCache` where possible.

---

## 4. Watch Cache Architecture

The watch cache (`pkg/storage/cacher/`) is fundamental to Kubernetes scalability. Without it, every controller's `WATCH` call would hit etcd directly, creating an unbounded fan-out.

### Structure

- **`Cacher`** (`cacher.go:262`) — the top-level struct; implements `storage.Interface` and acts as a transparent proxy, intercepting `Watch` and `List` calls.
- **`watchCache`** (`watch_cache.go`) — an ordered ring buffer of recent `watchCacheEvent`s, plus a snapshot store of all current objects for list serving.
- **`cacheWatcher`** (`cache_watcher.go`) — one instance per active client watch; receives events from the `Cacher`'s dispatch loop and filters them against the client's label/field selectors.

### Initialization Sequence

1. At startup, the `Cacher` performs a full `LIST` of its resource from etcd to populate the `watchCache` snapshot.
2. It records the `resourceVersion` from the list response.
3. It starts a background `WATCH` from that version, feeding events into the `incoming` channel (`cacher.go:268`).
4. A dispatch goroutine reads from `incoming`, updates the `watchCache` ring buffer, and fans out to all registered `cacheWatcher` instances.
5. The `ready` gate (`cacher.go:280`) blocks any client `List` or `Watch` until the initial list is complete.

### Bookmark Events

The cacher sends `BOOKMARK` events to each `cacheWatcher` to keep the `resourceVersion` field current even when no mutations are occurring. This prevents clients from drifting to a stale `resourceVersion` that would require a full `RELIST` from etcd on reconnect. Bookmarks are scheduled via `watcherBookmarkTimeBuckets` (`cacher.go:198`), with a default frequency of 1 minute (`defaultBookmarkFrequency`).

### Consistent Reads from Cache

For consistent reads (the default since 1.31), the cacher first fetches the current `mod_revision` from etcd (a cheap point-read), then waits for its cache to be at least that version before serving. This avoids stale reads without requiring a full etcd round-trip.

---

## 5. Server Composition: The Delegation Chain

The `kube-apiserver` binary composes three `GenericAPIServer` instances into a delegation chain. The `DelegationTarget` interface (`config.go:306-337`) defines how one server hands off to the next:

```
Aggregator Server
    ↓ (delegate)
Kube API Server (core)
    ↓ (delegate)
API Extensions Server (CRDs)
    ↓ (delegate)
emptyDelegate (returns 404)
```

Each server's `Handler.Director` checks its own registered routes. On a miss, `UnprotectedHandler()` (`genericapiserver.go:339`) returns the delegate server's `Director` as a fallback. This gives the aggregation layer first pick for extension API service paths, core APIs are handled next, and CRDs last.

The composition is wired in `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go`'s `CreateServerChain`.

---

## 6. Admission Control

Admission control lives in `pkg/admission/`. The key interfaces (`interfaces.go:122-173`) are:

```go
type MutationInterface interface {
    Interface
    Admit(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error
}

type ValidationInterface interface {
    Interface
    Validate(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error
}
```

The `chainAdmissionHandler` (`chain.go`) iterates the registered plugins in order. Mutation (`Admit`) runs first across all mutating plugins, then validation (`Validate`) runs across all validating plugins. A plugin declares which operations it handles via `Handles(operation Operation) bool`.

### Admission Attributes

`Attributes` (`interfaces.go:31-77`) is the interface passed to every plugin. It provides the object being created/updated, the old object (for updates/deletes), the GVK, the user info, dry-run status, and a mutable annotation map written to the audit log. Critically, `GetObject()` returns the already-decoded in-memory object—admission is the first and only stage where the body is materialized.

### Reinvocation

The admission chain supports reinvocation: if a mutating plugin sets `ShouldReinvoke()` on the `ReinvocationContext`, the entire mutation chain is re-run. This handles the case where plugin B depends on field state that plugin A sets.

### Webhook Support

The `plugin/` directory under `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/plugin/` contains the `MutatingAdmissionWebhook` and `ValidatingAdmissionWebhook` plugins. They serialize the `AdmissionReview` object, POST it to an external webhook endpoint, and interpret the response. Webhook plugins are themselves `MutationInterface`/`ValidationInterface` implementations that participate in the same chain.

---

## 7. Authentication and Authorization

### Authentication (`pkg/authentication/`)

The authenticator chain is a union of configured authenticators (`request/union/`). The first authenticator to return a non-nil `user.Info` wins. Common implementations include:
- `x509` — client certificate validation against a CA bundle
- `bearertoken` — validates a token string (delegates to a `TokenAuthenticator`)
- OIDC — validates JWTs from an external provider
- Service account tokens — issued by the apiserver's own OIDC-like endpoint

The authenticator is wired into the filter chain at `config.go:1056`:
```go
handler = genericapifilters.WithAuthentication(handler, c.Authentication.Authenticator, failedHandler, ...)
```

### Authorization (`pkg/authorization/`)

The authorizer chain uses a union-or-default pattern with three possible responses per authorizer: `Allow`, `Deny`, or `NoOpinion`. `NoOpinion` passes the request to the next authorizer. `Deny` short-circuits immediately. Only if all authorizers return `NoOpinion` is the request denied.

---

## 8. API Priority and Fairness (APF)

When `FlowControl` is configured, `WithPriorityAndFairness` (`pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go`) replaces the simpler `WithMaxInFlightLimit`. APF:

1. Classifies the request into a `FlowSchema` using the subject (user/group/service account) and resource.
2. Assigns the request to a `PriorityLevel` queue.
3. Estimates the request's "work" (`flowcontrolrequest.WorkEstimator`) based on the resource type and count of objects in storage.
4. Queues the request if the queue is full, or rejects it with HTTP 429 if the queue length limit is exceeded.

The work estimator uses `StorageObjectCountTracker.Get` to read the number of objects of a given type from the cache, enabling proportional queue sizing. The default fallback is the simple `WithMaxInFlightLimit` with limits of 400 read and 200 mutating requests.

---

## 9. Extension Points and Customization Hooks

### 9.1 `BuildHandlerChainFunc` — Filter Pipeline Replacement

```go
// config.go:429
BuildHandlerChainFunc: DefaultBuildHandlerChain,
```

Replacing this function allows embedding servers to add custom middleware globally or to disable specific standard filters. The function signature is `func(http.Handler, *Config) http.Handler`. **Risk**: omitting a standard filter (e.g., authorization) can create security holes. Adding filters that block or panic can make the server unresponsive.

### 9.2 `PostStartHooks` and `PreShutdownHooks`

```go
// hooks.go:43-46
type PostStartHookFunc func(context PostStartHookContext) error
type PreShutdownHookFunc func() error
```

Added via `Config.AddPostStartHook`. These run asynchronously in separate goroutines after the server starts listening. They receive a `PostStartHookContext` with a `LoopbackClientConfig` for authenticated self-calls. **Risk**: a hook that never completes or panics will consume a goroutine indefinitely; hooks that conflict (e.g., two hooks writing the same Lease) must implement their own coordination.

### 9.3 Health and Readiness Checks

```go
// config.go:570-580
func (c *Config) AddHealthChecks(healthChecks ...healthz.HealthChecker)
func (c *Config) AddReadyzChecks(healthChecks ...healthz.HealthChecker)
```

`healthz.HealthChecker` is a simple interface with `Name() string` and `Check(*http.Request) error`. Extension servers commonly add checks for their own custom dependencies (e.g., a secondary datastore). A failing readiness check prevents traffic from load balancers without killing the process.

### 9.4 REST Storage Interfaces — Capability Discovery

The `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` file defines a rich set of optional interfaces:

| Interface | Enables |
|---|---|
| `rest.Getter` | `GET /resource/{name}` |
| `rest.Lister` | `GET /resource` |
| `rest.Creater` | `POST /resource` |
| `rest.Updater` | `PUT /resource/{name}` |
| `rest.GracefulDeleter` | `DELETE /resource/{name}` with grace period |
| `rest.Watcher` | `GET /resource?watch=true` |
| `rest.Patcher` | `PATCH /resource/{name}` |
| `rest.TableConvertor` | Tabular output for `kubectl get` |
| `rest.StorageVersioner` | Storage version migration support |

The `APIInstaller` (`installer.go`) inspects the concrete storage object via type assertions at installation time to determine which HTTP verbs to register. Implementing only the interfaces you need keeps the API surface minimal.

### 9.5 `Store` Hook Functions

The `Store` struct exposes several hooks that are called at specific points in the CRUD lifecycle:

| Hook | Type | Called |
|---|---|---|
| `BeginCreate` | `BeginCreateFunc` | Before persistence; returns a commit/rollback func |
| `AfterCreate` | `AfterCreateFunc` | After successful persistence, before `Decorator` |
| `BeginUpdate` | `BeginUpdateFunc` | Before persistence |
| `AfterUpdate` | `AfterUpdateFunc` | After successful persistence |
| `AfterDelete` | `AfterDeleteFunc` | After deletion |
| `Decorator` | `func(runtime.Object)` | After every read from storage |
| `ShouldDeleteDuringUpdate` | `func(ctx, key, obj, existing) bool` | Whether an update should become a delete |

**Risk**: the `Decorator` is called on every read including from list/watch results. Heavy computation here directly increases latency under load.

### 9.6 Admission Plugins (`pkg/admission/`)

Adding an admission plugin requires:
1. Implementing `MutationInterface` and/or `ValidationInterface`.
2. Registering it with the plugin registry (`admission/plugins.go`).
3. Providing a `PluginInitializer` if the plugin needs shared resources (informers, clients).

`RecommendedOptions.ExtraAdmissionInitializers` provides the hook for extension API servers to inject their own initializers.

### 9.7 `RecommendedOptions` — Extension API Server Bootstrap

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/options/recommended.go` packages the most common configuration needs into a single struct:

```go
type RecommendedOptions struct {
    Etcd           *EtcdOptions
    SecureServing  *SecureServingOptionsWithLoopback
    Authentication *DelegatingAuthenticationOptions
    Authorization  *DelegatingAuthorizationOptions
    Audit          *AuditOptions
    Features       *FeatureOptions
    CoreAPI        *CoreAPIOptions
    Admission      *AdmissionOptions
    EgressSelector *EgressSelectorOptions
    Traces         *TracingOptions
}
```

`DelegatingAuthenticationOptions` and `DelegatingAuthorizationOptions` configure the server to delegate authn/authz decisions to the main `kube-apiserver` (via `SubjectAccessReview` and `TokenReview` APIs), which is the recommended pattern for extension servers.

---

## 10. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 10.1 etcd as the Single Point of Truth

All writes go through etcd. If etcd is unavailable:
- Mutating requests fail immediately (the etcd client returns an error; `Store.Create/Update/Delete` propagates a 500 or 503).
- Read requests _may_ still be served from the watch cache for `ResourceVersion=""` (best-effort) or for consistent reads if the cache has a recent-enough `ResourceVersion`.
- A prolonged etcd outage causes the watch cache to stall; the cacher's `ready` gate flips to `false` until the background watch reconnects and the cache is caught up.

### 10.2 Watch Cache Memory Pressure

The `watchCache` ring buffer retains events for `DefaultEventFreshDuration` (75 seconds by default, `cacher.go:72`). The number of watchers is bounded by available goroutines and memory. Each `cacheWatcher` has an input buffer channel; if a slow client cannot drain events fast enough, the `cacheWatcher` terminates the watch (forcing the client to relist). In clusters with many resources and many controllers, memory for watch caches can be measured in gigabytes.

### 10.3 Request Body Size Limits

The default `MaxRequestBodyBytes` is 3 MiB (`config.go:466`), matching the etcd recommended maximum. JSON payloads are larger than their proto equivalents; etcd stores proto. The comment in config explains the 2× factor for JSON-to-proto conversion during write. Exceeding the limit returns HTTP 413.

### 10.4 Optimistic Concurrency

Every object has a `resourceVersion` mapped to etcd's `mod_revision`. Updates must supply the current `resourceVersion`; a mismatch returns HTTP 409 Conflict. This is implemented in `storage/etcd3/store.go` using etcd's compare-and-swap (`txn(If(version == expected)).Then(Put(...))`). Clients must implement retry logic; the standard client-go `RetryOnConflict` helper exists for this.

### 10.5 Handler Chain Construction Ordering

`DefaultBuildHandlerChain` wraps handlers inside-out, so the last `handler = ...` line in the function executes first at request time (`WithAuditInit`). This ordering is subtle: tracing is placed _after_ authentication specifically to allow authenticated clients to influence sampling rates. Placing a filter in the wrong position (e.g., authorization before `WithRequestInfo` populates the context) would cause panics or incorrect behavior.

### 10.6 Graceful Shutdown Sequencing

The `lifecycle_signals.go` file documents the shutdown protocol precisely:

```
T0:    SIGTERM received → ShutdownInitiated → /readyz returns red
T0+70s: AfterShutdownDelayDuration → HTTP server stops accepting new connections
T0+130s: InFlightRequestsDrained (up to 60s ShutdownTimeout)
         → audit backend flushed → process exits
```

The 70-second default `ShutdownDelayDuration` exists to allow load balancer health checks to propagate before connections are dropped. During this window the server is still fully functional. Extension servers should use this same pattern by calling `GenericAPIServer.RunUntil`.

---

## 11. Operational Implications

### Performance

- **Serialization is the dominant CPU cost** for high-QPS read paths. The library uses proto encoding to etcd (via `StorageVersioner`) and JSON/proto for HTTP responses. Content type negotiation happens per request; `Accept: application/vnd.kubernetes.protobuf` reduces serialization load significantly for high-volume clients (controllers).
- **The `Decorator` hook runs on every read**. A `Decorator` that allocates or does I/O per object will dominate list latency for large collections. This is documented in `store.go:164`: "_Decorator is intended for integrations that are above storage and should only be used for specific cases where storage of the value is not appropriate, since they cannot be watched._"
- **Admission adds latency proportional to the number of webhooks**. Each webhook call is a synchronous HTTP POST to an external endpoint. A webhook with P99 latency of 100ms on a frequently mutated resource (e.g., pods) adds 100ms to every create/update.

### Reliability

- **Webhook timeouts must be configured** on `ValidatingWebhookConfiguration.timeoutSeconds` and `MutatingWebhookConfiguration.timeoutSeconds`. The default is 10 seconds. A webhook that consistently times out will block all writes to the targeted resource. `failurePolicy: Ignore` avoids this at the cost of missing enforcement.
- **APF misconfiguration is dangerous**. An overly restrictive `PriorityLevel` that starves `system` or `leader-election` priority classes can prevent the cluster from recovering from failures. APF configuration is itself stored in etcd, so a bootstrap deadlock is possible if the server cannot read its own flow control config.
- **The `StorageReadinessHook`** (`server/storage_readiness_hook.go`) blocks the `readyz` endpoint until all registered storages report ready. Extension servers that register storage via `InstallAPIGroups` get this for free.

### Maintainability

- **Code-generating strategies reduces drift**. `deepcopy-gen`, `conversion-gen`, and `defaulter-gen` produce the boilerplate needed by the `Scheme`. Running generators on every type change is critical; skipping them causes runtime panics (nil pointer in DeepCopy) or silent data loss (missing conversion paths).
- **`RecommendedOptions.ApplyTo` is the correct entry point** for extension servers, not raw `Config`. It applies sensible defaults and wires delegating authn/authz automatically.
- **The hub-and-spoke version model** means every new API version requires an explicit conversion to/from the internal hub version. Failing to provide a conversion function causes HTTP 406 Not Acceptable when a client requests an unhandled version.

---

## 12. File and Directory Map

The following files are most important for understanding and extending the apiserver library:

| File / Directory | Role |
|---|---|
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Official high-level architecture document with Mermaid diagrams |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/config.go` | `Config` struct, `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`, server defaults |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` | `GenericAPIServer`, `APIGroupInfo`, `InstallAPIGroups`, `DelegationTarget` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/handler.go` | `APIServerHandler` struct and dual-router architecture |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/hooks.go` | `PostStartHookFunc`, `PreShutdownHookFunc` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go` | Shutdown sequence documentation and signal types |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/options/recommended.go` | `RecommendedOptions` for extension API server bootstrap |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/installer.go` | `APIInstaller` — maps REST storage to HTTP routes |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go` | Full CREATE request lifecycle, admission, decode, storage |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go` | Authentication filter |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go` | Authorization filter |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` | All REST storage capability interfaces |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go` | `Store` CRUD+Watch engine, strategy hooks |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/interfaces.go` | `storage.Interface`, `Versioner`, `UpdateFunc` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go` | `Cacher`, watch fan-out, bookmark scheduling |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go` | Ring-buffer event store |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go` | etcd3 backend implementation |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/interfaces.go` | `MutationInterface`, `ValidationInterface`, `Attributes` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/chain.go` | `chainAdmissionHandler` iteration logic |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go` | APF request dispatch |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/filters/maxinflight.go` | Simple in-flight limit fallback |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/filters/timeout.go` | Non-long-running request timeout enforcement |

# Kubernetes API Server Library: Subsystem Architecture and Extension Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

The `k8s.io/apiserver` library (`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`) is the reusable foundation upon which every Kubernetes API server is built. It is not the full `kube-apiserver` binary—it is the generic scaffolding that provides the HTTP serving infrastructure, request filtering pipeline, type-system integration, storage abstraction, and security plumbing. A consumer of this library instantiates a `GenericAPIServer`, attaches REST storage implementations behind `APIGroupInfo` descriptors, and gets a standards-compliant Kubernetes API surface for free.

### Upstream Dependencies

The library sits on top of:

- **`k8s.io/apimachinery`** — type system primitives (`runtime.Scheme`, `runtime.Object`, `GroupVersionKind`, codec infrastructure, field/label selectors)
- **`k8s.io/client-go`** — informers and the REST client used by the loopback client and optional webhook callers
- **etcd (via `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3`)** — the sole officially-supported storage backend, accessed through `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/etcd3/`
- **`k8s.io/kube-openapi`** — OpenAPI v2/v3 spec generation and serving

### Downstream Consumers

- **`kube-apiserver` core** (`pkg/controlplane/`) — registers built-in API groups by calling `GenericAPIServer.InstallLegacyAPIGroup` / `InstallAPIGroups`
- **`kube-aggregator`** — builds an aggregation-layer server on top of the library
- **`apiextensions-apiserver`** — uses the library to serve CRDs
- **Extension API servers** built with `apiserver-builder` or manually using `RecommendedOptions`

### Boundaries

The library handles everything between network bytes and your storage call: TLS termination, authentication, authorization, flow control, serialisation, admission, versioning, and watch fan-out. It does **not** own cluster-level policy (that is RBAC controller code in `pkg/`), controller logic, or the etcd cluster itself.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

The directory structure maps almost one-to-one to functional responsibility:

| Directory | Primary Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `pkg/server/` | Server bootstrap: `Config`, `GenericAPIServer`, handler chain wiring, lifecycle signals, hooks |
| `pkg/server/options/` | CLI flag binding; `RecommendedOptions` composes all option groups for extension API servers |
| `pkg/endpoints/` | HTTP-level dispatch: `APIInstaller`, verb handlers (`create.go`, `get.go`, `patch.go`, `watch.go`), filters |
| `pkg/registry/rest/` | REST storage interfaces (`rest.Storage`, `Getter`, `Creater`, `Updater`, etc.) |
| `pkg/registry/generic/registry/` | Generic `Store` struct implementing CRUD+Watch on top of `storage.Interface` |
| `pkg/storage/` | Storage interface definition and `Versioner` abstraction |
| `pkg/storage/cacher/` | In-memory watch cache (`Cacher`, `watchCache`, `cacheWatcher`) |
| `pkg/storage/etcd3/` | etcd3 implementation of `storage.Interface` |
| `pkg/admission/` | Admission plugin interfaces, chain, plugin registry, initializers |
| `pkg/authentication/` | Pluggable authenticator chain |
| `pkg/authorization/` | Pluggable authorizer chain |
| `pkg/audit/` | Policy-driven audit event pipeline |
| `pkg/util/flowcontrol/` | API Priority and Fairness (APF): `FlowSchema`, `PriorityLevel`, work estimator |

### The `GenericAPIServer` and `Config`

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` contains the `GenericAPIServer` struct: the central object at runtime. It owns the `APIServerHandler` (the full HTTP handler chain), the discovery endpoint managers, health check registries, the audit backend, and the `delegationTarget` pointer for the server chain.

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/config.go` holds the `Config` struct, which is populated from option structs and then "completed" (via `Config.Complete()`) before being used to construct the server. Default values are set in `NewConfig`:

```go
// config.go:427-478
return &Config{
    BuildHandlerChainFunc:          DefaultBuildHandlerChain,
    MaxRequestsInFlight:            400,
    MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight:    200,
    RequestTimeout:                 time.Duration(60) * time.Second,
    JSONPatchMaxCopyBytes:          int64(3 * 1024 * 1024),
    MaxRequestBodyBytes:            int64(3 * 1024 * 1024),
    ...
}
```

The field `BuildHandlerChainFunc` is the primary extension point for the filter pipeline—it defaults to `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` but callers may replace it entirely.

### `APIGroupInfo` and REST Storage Registration

An API group is attached to the server by constructing an `APIGroupInfo` and calling `InstallAPIGroups`:

```go
// genericapiserver.go:71-98
type APIGroupInfo struct {
    PrioritizedVersions          []schema.GroupVersion
    VersionedResourcesStorageMap map[string]map[string]rest.Storage
    Scheme                       *runtime.Scheme
    NegotiatedSerializer         runtime.NegotiatedSerializer
    ParameterCodec               runtime.ParameterCodec
    ...
}
```

The `VersionedResourcesStorageMap` maps `version → resource-name → rest.Storage`. `InstallAPIGroups` (`genericapiserver.go:876`) iterates the versions, calls `installAPIResources`, and registers discovery handlers. `InstallLegacyAPIGroup` does the same for the `/api` (core) prefix.

### The `Store` — Generic CRUD Engine

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go` contains the `Store` struct, which is the standard CRUD/Watch implementation. Every built-in resource embeds a `Store`. It delegates to a `DryRunnableStorage` (wrapping `storage.Interface`) and enforces business logic through strategy objects:

```go
// store.go:100-248 (selected fields)
type Store struct {
    CreateStrategy rest.RESTCreateStrategy
    UpdateStrategy rest.RESTUpdateStrategy
    DeleteStrategy rest.RESTDeleteStrategy
    BeginCreate    BeginCreateFunc   // optional "transaction" hook
    AfterCreate    AfterCreateFunc   // optional post-create hook
    BeginUpdate    BeginUpdateFunc
    AfterUpdate    AfterUpdateFunc
    AfterDelete    AfterDeleteFunc
    Decorator      func(runtime.Object) // exit hook after storage read
    Storage        DryRunnableStorage
    ...
}
```

Strategies carry the actual business logic (field immutability, name generation, namespace defaulting, validation). The `Store` itself is storage-agnostic; it calls the strategy before writing and after reading.

### The `APIServerHandler` — Routing Architecture

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/handler.go` describes the dual-routing system:

```
FullHandlerChain → Director → GoRestfulContainer (API paths)
                            → NonGoRestfulMux    (utility paths: /healthz, /metrics, /openapi)
```

`GoRestfulContainer` (from `github.com/emicklei/go-restful`) handles structured REST API routing with content negotiation. `NonGoRestfulMux` is a simple `PathRecorderMux` for everything else. The `Director` inspects the incoming path to decide which branch to use.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

This section traces a `kubectl create deployment` (a `POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments`) through the full stack.

### Phase 1: Outer Filter Chain (`DefaultBuildHandlerChain`)

The filter chain is assembled inside-out (wrappers applied in reverse order of the listed sequence). The code in `config.go:1015-1096` shows the exact wrap order. When a request arrives, it traverses these filters from outermost to innermost:

1. **`WithAuditInit`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit_init.go`) — allocates an audit event for the request.
2. **`WithPanicRecovery`** (`pkg/server/filters/wrap.go`) — catches panics, converts them to 500s.
3. **`WithMuxAndDiscoveryComplete`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/mux_discovery_complete.go`) — blocks requests until all API paths are installed (prevents spurious 404s at startup).
4. **`WithRequestReceivedTimestamp`** — stamps the request receipt time.
5. **`WithRequestInfo`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/requestinfo.go`) — parses the URL into a `RequestInfo` struct (verb, resource, namespace, name) and injects it into the context.
6. **`WithRoutine`** (optional, feature-gated) — executes downstream handlers in a new goroutine to minimize stack depth.
7. **`WithLatencyTrackers`** / **`WithHTTPLogging`** — metrics and structured logging.
8. **`WithRetryAfter`** (optional) — rejects new requests with HTTP 429 during graceful shutdown.
9. **`WithHSTS`** / **`WithCacheControl`** — response header policies.
10. **`WithProbabilisticGoaway`** (optional) — probabilistically sends `GOAWAY` on HTTP/2 connections to trigger client reconnection and load rebalancing.
11. **`WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown`** / **`WithWaitGroup`** — tracks in-flight requests for graceful drain; rate-limits watch draining during shutdown.
12. **`WithRequestDeadline`** — applies the request timeout from `Config.RequestTimeout` (default 60s) to the context.
13. **`WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests`** — enforces the deadline by running downstream in a goroutine and returning a timeout response if the deadline is exceeded.
14. **`WithWarningRecorder`** — thread-safe buffer for adding warning headers to the response.
15. **`WithAuthentication`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go`) — runs the authenticator chain; on failure, invokes the `failedHandler` which returns `401 Unauthorized`.
16. **`WithTracing`** (optional, feature-gated) — starts an OpenTelemetry span; placed after authn so clients can influence sampling.
17. **`WithAudit`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit.go`) — populates audit event with user identity and response status.
18. **`WithImpersonation`** / **`WithConstrainedImpersonation`** — checks and applies user impersonation (`Impersonate-*` headers).
19. **`WithPriorityAndFairness`** or **`WithMaxInFlightLimit`** — either APF-based flow control (classifies request into a `FlowSchema`/`PriorityLevel` queue) or the simpler fixed limit (`MaxRequestsInFlight=400`, `MaxMutatingRequestsInFlight=200`).
20. **`WithAuthorization`** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go`) — invokes the authorizer chain; returns `403 Forbidden` on denial.

At this point the request is authenticated, authorized, and admitted through flow control. The body has still **not** been deserialized.

### Phase 2: REST Endpoint Handler (`pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go`)

The `APIInstaller` (`pkg/endpoints/installer.go`) registered an HTTP handler for `POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments` when `InstallAPIGroups` was called. That handler is `createHandler` in `pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go`.

Inside `createHandler`:

1. **Content negotiation** — selects the input and output serializer based on `Content-Type` / `Accept` headers (`negotiation.NegotiateInputSerializer`).
2. **Body read** — `limitedReadBodyWithRecordMetric` reads up to `MaxRequestBodyBytes` (3 MiB by default).
3. **Decode** — `scope.Serializer.DecoderToVersion(decodeSerializer, scope.HubGroupVersion)` converts the wire JSON/YAML/protobuf bytes into the internal version of the object via the `Scheme`'s hub-and-spoke conversion.
4. **Field validation** — if `FieldValidation=Warn` or `Strict`, a strict decoder is used and unknown fields produce warnings or errors.
5. **Admission (mutation phase)** — `admission.NewAttributesRecord(obj, ...)` creates the `Attributes` package; `requestFunc` wraps the final storage call. The field manager (`FieldManager.UpdateNoErrors`) sets `managedFields`.
6. **`finisher.FinishRequest`** — runs the actual storage call and admission in a dedicated goroutine with the request context deadline.
7. **Admission (validation phase)** — `ManagedFieldsValidatingAdmissionController` validates field manager annotations; then the generic admission chain's `Validate` pass runs.
8. **Storage write** — `r.Create(ctx, name, obj, validateFunc, options)` calls the `Store.Create` method, which invokes `CreateStrategy.PrepareForCreate`, `CreateStrategy.Validate`, then `Storage.Create` to write to etcd via `GuaranteedUpdate` (or the cacher).
9. **Response** — the stored object is serialized to the negotiated output format and written to `http.ResponseWriter`.

### Phase 3: Storage Layer

The storage call descends through:

```
Store.Storage (DryRunnableStorage)
  → cacher.Cacher (implements storage.Interface)
      → etcd3.store (implements storage.Interface)
          → etcd grpc client
```

For **writes**, the cacher delegates directly to the etcd3 store. For **reads and watches**, the cacher serves from its in-memory `watchCache` where possible.

---

## 4. Watch Cache Architecture

The watch cache (`pkg/storage/cacher/`) is fundamental to Kubernetes scalability. Without it, every controller's `WATCH` call would hit etcd directly, creating an unbounded fan-out.

### Structure

- **`Cacher`** (`cacher.go:262`) — the top-level struct; implements `storage.Interface` and acts as a transparent proxy, intercepting `Watch` and `List` calls.
- **`watchCache`** (`watch_cache.go`) — an ordered ring buffer of recent `watchCacheEvent`s, plus a snapshot store of all current objects for list serving.
- **`cacheWatcher`** (`cache_watcher.go`) — one instance per active client watch; receives events from the `Cacher`'s dispatch loop and filters them against the client's label/field selectors.

### Initialization Sequence

1. At startup, the `Cacher` performs a full `LIST` of its resource from etcd to populate the `watchCache` snapshot.
2. It records the `resourceVersion` from the list response.
3. It starts a background `WATCH` from that version, feeding events into the `incoming` channel (`cacher.go:268`).
4. A dispatch goroutine reads from `incoming`, updates the `watchCache` ring buffer, and fans out to all registered `cacheWatcher` instances.
5. The `ready` gate (`cacher.go:280`) blocks any client `List` or `Watch` until the initial list is complete.

### Bookmark Events

The cacher sends `BOOKMARK` events to each `cacheWatcher` to keep the `resourceVersion` field current even when no mutations are occurring. This prevents clients from drifting to a stale `resourceVersion` that would require a full `RELIST` from etcd on reconnect. Bookmarks are scheduled via `watcherBookmarkTimeBuckets` (`cacher.go:198`), with a default frequency of 1 minute (`defaultBookmarkFrequency`).

### Consistent Reads from Cache

For consistent reads (the default since 1.31), the cacher first fetches the current `mod_revision` from etcd (a cheap point-read), then waits for its cache to be at least that version before serving. This avoids stale reads without requiring a full etcd round-trip.

---

## 5. Server Composition: The Delegation Chain

The `kube-apiserver` binary composes three `GenericAPIServer` instances into a delegation chain. The `DelegationTarget` interface (`config.go:306-337`) defines how one server hands off to the next:

```
Aggregator Server
    ↓ (delegate)
Kube API Server (core)
    ↓ (delegate)
API Extensions Server (CRDs)
    ↓ (delegate)
emptyDelegate (returns 404)
```

Each server's `Handler.Director` checks its own registered routes. On a miss, `UnprotectedHandler()` (`genericapiserver.go:339`) returns the delegate server's `Director` as a fallback. This gives the aggregation layer first pick for extension API service paths, core APIs are handled next, and CRDs last.

The composition is wired in `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go`'s `CreateServerChain`.

---

## 6. Admission Control

Admission control lives in `pkg/admission/`. The key interfaces (`interfaces.go:122-173`) are:

```go
type MutationInterface interface {
    Interface
    Admit(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error
}

type ValidationInterface interface {
    Interface
    Validate(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error
}
```

The `chainAdmissionHandler` (`chain.go`) iterates the registered plugins in order. Mutation (`Admit`) runs first across all mutating plugins, then validation (`Validate`) runs across all validating plugins. A plugin declares which operations it handles via `Handles(operation Operation) bool`.

### Admission Attributes

`Attributes` (`interfaces.go:31-77`) is the interface passed to every plugin. It provides the object being created/updated, the old object (for updates/deletes), the GVK, the user info, dry-run status, and a mutable annotation map written to the audit log. Critically, `GetObject()` returns the already-decoded in-memory object—admission is the first and only stage where the body is materialized.

### Reinvocation

The admission chain supports reinvocation: if a mutating plugin sets `ShouldReinvoke()` on the `ReinvocationContext`, the entire mutation chain is re-run. This handles the case where plugin B depends on field state that plugin A sets.

### Webhook Support

The `plugin/` directory under `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/plugin/` contains the `MutatingAdmissionWebhook` and `ValidatingAdmissionWebhook` plugins. They serialize the `AdmissionReview` object, POST it to an external webhook endpoint, and interpret the response. Webhook plugins are themselves `MutationInterface`/`ValidationInterface` implementations that participate in the same chain.

---

## 7. Authentication and Authorization

### Authentication (`pkg/authentication/`)

The authenticator chain is a union of configured authenticators (`request/union/`). The first authenticator to return a non-nil `user.Info` wins. Common implementations include:
- `x509` — client certificate validation against a CA bundle
- `bearertoken` — validates a token string (delegates to a `TokenAuthenticator`)
- OIDC — validates JWTs from an external provider
- Service account tokens — issued by the apiserver's own OIDC-like endpoint

The authenticator is wired into the filter chain at `config.go:1056`:
```go
handler = genericapifilters.WithAuthentication(handler, c.Authentication.Authenticator, failedHandler, ...)
```

### Authorization (`pkg/authorization/`)

The authorizer chain uses a union-or-default pattern with three possible responses per authorizer: `Allow`, `Deny`, or `NoOpinion`. `NoOpinion` passes the request to the next authorizer. `Deny` short-circuits immediately. Only if all authorizers return `NoOpinion` is the request denied.

---

## 8. API Priority and Fairness (APF)

When `FlowControl` is configured, `WithPriorityAndFairness` (`pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go`) replaces the simpler `WithMaxInFlightLimit`. APF:

1. Classifies the request into a `FlowSchema` using the subject (user/group/service account) and resource.
2. Assigns the request to a `PriorityLevel` queue.
3. Estimates the request's "work" (`flowcontrolrequest.WorkEstimator`) based on the resource type and count of objects in storage.
4. Queues the request if the queue is full, or rejects it with HTTP 429 if the queue length limit is exceeded.

The work estimator uses `StorageObjectCountTracker.Get` to read the number of objects of a given type from the cache, enabling proportional queue sizing. The default fallback is the simple `WithMaxInFlightLimit` with limits of 400 read and 200 mutating requests.

---

## 9. Extension Points and Customization Hooks

### 9.1 `BuildHandlerChainFunc` — Filter Pipeline Replacement

```go
// config.go:429
BuildHandlerChainFunc: DefaultBuildHandlerChain,
```

Replacing this function allows embedding servers to add custom middleware globally or to disable specific standard filters. The function signature is `func(http.Handler, *Config) http.Handler`. **Risk**: omitting a standard filter (e.g., authorization) can create security holes. Adding filters that block or panic can make the server unresponsive.

### 9.2 `PostStartHooks` and `PreShutdownHooks`

```go
// hooks.go:43-46
type PostStartHookFunc func(context PostStartHookContext) error
type PreShutdownHookFunc func() error
```

Added via `Config.AddPostStartHook`. These run asynchronously in separate goroutines after the server starts listening. They receive a `PostStartHookContext` with a `LoopbackClientConfig` for authenticated self-calls. **Risk**: a hook that never completes or panics will consume a goroutine indefinitely; hooks that conflict (e.g., two hooks writing the same Lease) must implement their own coordination.

### 9.3 Health and Readiness Checks

```go
// config.go:570-580
func (c *Config) AddHealthChecks(healthChecks ...healthz.HealthChecker)
func (c *Config) AddReadyzChecks(healthChecks ...healthz.HealthChecker)
```

`healthz.HealthChecker` is a simple interface with `Name() string` and `Check(*http.Request) error`. Extension servers commonly add checks for their own custom dependencies (e.g., a secondary datastore). A failing readiness check prevents traffic from load balancers without killing the process.

### 9.4 REST Storage Interfaces — Capability Discovery

The `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` file defines a rich set of optional interfaces:

| Interface | Enables |
|---|---|
| `rest.Getter` | `GET /resource/{name}` |
| `rest.Lister` | `GET /resource` |
| `rest.Creater` | `POST /resource` |
| `rest.Updater` | `PUT /resource/{name}` |
| `rest.GracefulDeleter` | `DELETE /resource/{name}` with grace period |
| `rest.Watcher` | `GET /resource?watch=true` |
| `rest.Patcher` | `PATCH /resource/{name}` |
| `rest.TableConvertor` | Tabular output for `kubectl get` |
| `rest.StorageVersioner` | Storage version migration support |

The `APIInstaller` (`installer.go`) inspects the concrete storage object via type assertions at installation time to determine which HTTP verbs to register. Implementing only the interfaces you need keeps the API surface minimal.

### 9.5 `Store` Hook Functions

The `Store` struct exposes several hooks that are called at specific points in the CRUD lifecycle:

| Hook | Type | Called |
|---|---|---|
| `BeginCreate` | `BeginCreateFunc` | Before persistence; returns a commit/rollback func |
| `AfterCreate` | `AfterCreateFunc` | After successful persistence, before `Decorator` |
| `BeginUpdate` | `BeginUpdateFunc` | Before persistence |
| `AfterUpdate` | `AfterUpdateFunc` | After successful persistence |
| `AfterDelete` | `AfterDeleteFunc` | After deletion |
| `Decorator` | `func(runtime.Object)` | After every read from storage |
| `ShouldDeleteDuringUpdate` | `func(ctx, key, obj, existing) bool` | Whether an update should become a delete |

**Risk**: the `Decorator` is called on every read including from list/watch results. Heavy computation here directly increases latency under load.

### 9.6 Admission Plugins (`pkg/admission/`)

Adding an admission plugin requires:
1. Implementing `MutationInterface` and/or `ValidationInterface`.
2. Registering it with the plugin registry (`admission/plugins.go`).
3. Providing a `PluginInitializer` if the plugin needs shared resources (informers, clients).

`RecommendedOptions.ExtraAdmissionInitializers` provides the hook for extension API servers to inject their own initializers.

### 9.7 `RecommendedOptions` — Extension API Server Bootstrap

`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/options/recommended.go` packages the most common configuration needs into a single struct:

```go
type RecommendedOptions struct {
    Etcd           *EtcdOptions
    SecureServing  *SecureServingOptionsWithLoopback
    Authentication *DelegatingAuthenticationOptions
    Authorization  *DelegatingAuthorizationOptions
    Audit          *AuditOptions
    Features       *FeatureOptions
    CoreAPI        *CoreAPIOptions
    Admission      *AdmissionOptions
    EgressSelector *EgressSelectorOptions
    Traces         *TracingOptions
}
```

`DelegatingAuthenticationOptions` and `DelegatingAuthorizationOptions` configure the server to delegate authn/authz decisions to the main `kube-apiserver` (via `SubjectAccessReview` and `TokenReview` APIs), which is the recommended pattern for extension servers.

---

## 10. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 10.1 etcd as the Single Point of Truth

All writes go through etcd. If etcd is unavailable:
- Mutating requests fail immediately (the etcd client returns an error; `Store.Create/Update/Delete` propagates a 500 or 503).
- Read requests _may_ still be served from the watch cache for `ResourceVersion=""` (best-effort) or for consistent reads if the cache has a recent-enough `ResourceVersion`.
- A prolonged etcd outage causes the watch cache to stall; the cacher's `ready` gate flips to `false` until the background watch reconnects and the cache is caught up.

### 10.2 Watch Cache Memory Pressure

The `watchCache` ring buffer retains events for `DefaultEventFreshDuration` (75 seconds by default, `cacher.go:72`). The number of watchers is bounded by available goroutines and memory. Each `cacheWatcher` has an input buffer channel; if a slow client cannot drain events fast enough, the `cacheWatcher` terminates the watch (forcing the client to relist). In clusters with many resources and many controllers, memory for watch caches can be measured in gigabytes.

### 10.3 Request Body Size Limits

The default `MaxRequestBodyBytes` is 3 MiB (`config.go:466`), matching the etcd recommended maximum. JSON payloads are larger than their proto equivalents; etcd stores proto. The comment in config explains the 2× factor for JSON-to-proto conversion during write. Exceeding the limit returns HTTP 413.

### 10.4 Optimistic Concurrency

Every object has a `resourceVersion` mapped to etcd's `mod_revision`. Updates must supply the current `resourceVersion`; a mismatch returns HTTP 409 Conflict. This is implemented in `storage/etcd3/store.go` using etcd's compare-and-swap (`txn(If(version == expected)).Then(Put(...))`). Clients must implement retry logic; the standard client-go `RetryOnConflict` helper exists for this.

### 10.5 Handler Chain Construction Ordering

`DefaultBuildHandlerChain` wraps handlers inside-out, so the last `handler = ...` line in the function executes first at request time (`WithAuditInit`). This ordering is subtle: tracing is placed _after_ authentication specifically to allow authenticated clients to influence sampling rates. Placing a filter in the wrong position (e.g., authorization before `WithRequestInfo` populates the context) would cause panics or incorrect behavior.

### 10.6 Graceful Shutdown Sequencing

The `lifecycle_signals.go` file documents the shutdown protocol precisely:

```
T0:    SIGTERM received → ShutdownInitiated → /readyz returns red
T0+70s: AfterShutdownDelayDuration → HTTP server stops accepting new connections
T0+130s: InFlightRequestsDrained (up to 60s ShutdownTimeout)
         → audit backend flushed → process exits
```

The 70-second default `ShutdownDelayDuration` exists to allow load balancer health checks to propagate before connections are dropped. During this window the server is still fully functional. Extension servers should use this same pattern by calling `GenericAPIServer.RunUntil`.

---

## 11. Operational Implications

### Performance

- **Serialization is the dominant CPU cost** for high-QPS read paths. The library uses proto encoding to etcd (via `StorageVersioner`) and JSON/proto for HTTP responses. Content type negotiation happens per request; `Accept: application/vnd.kubernetes.protobuf` reduces serialization load significantly for high-volume clients (controllers).
- **The `Decorator` hook runs on every read**. A `Decorator` that allocates or does I/O per object will dominate list latency for large collections. This is documented in `store.go:164`: "_Decorator is intended for integrations that are above storage and should only be used for specific cases where storage of the value is not appropriate, since they cannot be watched._"
- **Admission adds latency proportional to the number of webhooks**. Each webhook call is a synchronous HTTP POST to an external endpoint. A webhook with P99 latency of 100ms on a frequently mutated resource (e.g., pods) adds 100ms to every create/update.

### Reliability

- **Webhook timeouts must be configured** on `ValidatingWebhookConfiguration.timeoutSeconds` and `MutatingWebhookConfiguration.timeoutSeconds`. The default is 10 seconds. A webhook that consistently times out will block all writes to the targeted resource. `failurePolicy: Ignore` avoids this at the cost of missing enforcement.
- **APF misconfiguration is dangerous**. An overly restrictive `PriorityLevel` that starves `system` or `leader-election` priority classes can prevent the cluster from recovering from failures. APF configuration is itself stored in etcd, so a bootstrap deadlock is possible if the server cannot read its own flow control config.
- **The `StorageReadinessHook`** (`server/storage_readiness_hook.go`) blocks the `readyz` endpoint until all registered storages report ready. Extension servers that register storage via `InstallAPIGroups` get this for free.

### Maintainability

- **Code-generating strategies reduces drift**. `deepcopy-gen`, `conversion-gen`, and `defaulter-gen` produce the boilerplate needed by the `Scheme`. Running generators on every type change is critical; skipping them causes runtime panics (nil pointer in DeepCopy) or silent data loss (missing conversion paths).
- **`RecommendedOptions.ApplyTo` is the correct entry point** for extension servers, not raw `Config`. It applies sensible defaults and wires delegating authn/authz automatically.
- **The hub-and-spoke version model** means every new API version requires an explicit conversion to/from the internal hub version. Failing to provide a conversion function causes HTTP 406 Not Acceptable when a client requests an unhandled version.

---

## 12. File and Directory Map

The following files are most important for understanding and extending the apiserver library:

| File / Directory | Role |
|---|---|
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Official high-level architecture document with Mermaid diagrams |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/config.go` | `Config` struct, `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`, server defaults |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` | `GenericAPIServer`, `APIGroupInfo`, `InstallAPIGroups`, `DelegationTarget` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/handler.go` | `APIServerHandler` struct and dual-router architecture |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/hooks.go` | `PostStartHookFunc`, `PreShutdownHookFunc` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go` | Shutdown sequence documentation and signal types |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/options/recommended.go` | `RecommendedOptions` for extension API server bootstrap |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/installer.go` | `APIInstaller` — maps REST storage to HTTP routes |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go` | Full CREATE request lifecycle, admission, decode, storage |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go` | Authentication filter |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go` | Authorization filter |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` | All REST storage capability interfaces |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go` | `Store` CRUD+Watch engine, strategy hooks |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/interfaces.go` | `storage.Interface`, `Versioner`, `UpdateFunc` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go` | `Cacher`, watch fan-out, bookmark scheduling |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go` | Ring-buffer event store |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go` | etcd3 backend implementation |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/interfaces.go` | `MutationInterface`, `ValidationInterface`, `Attributes` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/chain.go` | `chainAdmissionHandler` iteration logic |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go` | APF request dispatch |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/filters/maxinflight.go` | Simple in-flight limit fallback |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/filters/timeout.go` | Non-long-running request timeout enforcement |
